title: The Name on the Ledger
special_element: The entry predates the character's arrival by a generation, in a hand that is not theirs — yet the name is spelled in the exact private form their family uses at home, the form no one outside the household has ever known.
summary: The Hollow Ward Post smells of old paper and something sweet, like candle-wax left too long in the sun.
body: The Hollow Ward Post smells of old paper and something sweet, like candle-wax left too long in the sun. The duty ledger lies open on the desk — entries in a dozen different hands, the oldest faded to rust-brown. Near the bottom, in ink that has not yet dried, is your name.

Special element: The entry predates the character's arrival by a generation, in a hand that is not theirs — yet the name is spelled in the exact private form their family uses at home, the form no one outside the household has ever known.
next_steps: **Stakes:** The ledger is not a list of who passed through — it is a list of who is *owed for*. Your name is in the debt column, entered a generation before you were born, in your family's private hand. A duty was promised in your name, and the post has kept your place. Now you have arrived, and the entry beside your name has begun to dry — as though someone is finally ready to collect.

**Next steps:** Find out what was promised, and by whom — a name in the signing column the family will not discuss. Decide whether to pay the duty, refuse it (the post does not take refusal well), or learn what the post actually is before you do either.

**Twists:** A second entry, directly below, reads *departed* — dated tomorrow. The signing hand in the column is one the character will recognise the first time they see a family document — it is their own, not yet written.

title: Ferryman's Fare
special_element: Old Marrow never wanted money. The coins go into the river — they always have — and what the ferryman truly collects is a **secret, spoken aloud on the crossing, so the water can hear it.** The PC paid in metal and never paid the real fare. Until they return to that bank, find that ferryman, and speak the secret owed, the coin keeps coming back — cold and waiting, wherever they sleep.
summary: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 1 · Mood: mysterious
body: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 1 · Mood: mysterious

You crossed a river once — grey morning, the water swollen and cold, a ferryman who had your name on his tongue before you gave it. You counted out the fare. He took it without looking, watched you reach the far bank in a voice like rope dragged over wet wood, and never once looked at the water.

That was a season ago. Last night, before dawn, someone left a single coin on your windowsill. It was cold to the touch, the way only river-water is cold. Your name was on one face — not printed, not stamped. Scratched in, like a reminder.

Special element: Old Marrow never wanted money. The coins go into the river — they always have — and what the ferryman truly collects is a **secret, spoken aloud on the crossing, so the water can hear it.** The PC paid in metal and never paid the real fare. Until they return to that bank, find that ferryman, and speak the secret owed, the coin keeps coming back — cold and waiting, wherever they sleep.
next_steps: **Stakes:** The debt is quiet for now — a coin, a chill. But it is a marker, and something on the far side of every crossing knows where the PC stands.
**Next steps:** Return to the crossing and name the secret. Or don't — and learn what the river does when a toll runs long overdue.
Marrow doesn't want a shameful secret. He wants the one true thing the PC has never said aloud to anyone — and saying it, to the river, changes something in them. The DM decides what.
The crossing the PC remembers may not be the one they took. Marrow ferried someone with their face the same morning.
If the PC has already met Marrow's Toll, they may recognise this coin in the river-tally — and understand, with sick clarity, what they're being counted toward.

title: The Memory the Saltwidow Took
special_element: The stolen year isn't sitting frozen in the Saltwidow's locket — it's still *living* inside it, one remembered hour at a time, unfolding exactly as it would have. The child in there is growing up without the party. Every month the PC delays, the person they were drifts further from who they'd have become. By the time the locket finally opens, it may not give back the child who was taken. It may give back a stranger the PC can't recognize as themselves.
summary: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 3 · Mood: melancholy
body: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 3 · Mood: melancholy

Somewhere between your ninth and tenth birthdays, a whole year is missing. Ask anyone who knew you then and they go quiet, or they call you "different that year" and won't say how. You don't remember it either — not one day of it. Only a smell of cold coast-salt that catches you off guard sometimes, on a dry street, miles from any water, for no reason you can name.

Special element: The stolen year isn't sitting frozen in the Saltwidow's locket — it's still *living* inside it, one remembered hour at a time, unfolding exactly as it would have. The child in there is growing up without the party. Every month the PC delays, the person they were drifts further from who they'd have become. By the time the locket finally opens, it may not give back the child who was taken. It may give back a stranger the PC can't recognize as themselves.
next_steps: Stakes: the longer the PC waits, the further the memory-self diverges — the year they get back is not the year that was taken.
Next steps: word reaches the party that the Saltwidow trades favours for memories; finding her, and finding her price for this one, becomes the thread.
Twists: the price isn't gold or a quest — it's a *current* memory, freely given, of equal weight to the one she'd return; and she already knows exactly which one she wants.

title: The Oath You Inherited
special_element: The scar is the last living thread of Ser Aldous the Unfallen's first oath — the one he swore whole, generations before his first death, before it all started going wrong — carried down through blood he never knew he left behind. It has slept four lifetimes. The first time this character is dying and would not last the hour, the oath finishes what Aldous never could: it speaks once, in his exact final vow, through the character's own mouth, and it saves them — and from that moment binds them to end what Aldous became.
summary: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 4 · Mood: heroic
body: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 4 · Mood: heroic

There is a hairline scar along your sword hand that no blade ever made — pale as old wax, running *with* the vein instead of across it. On the nights you can't sleep, you taste iron and candle smoke, and hear, very faint, a vow being spoken in a voice that is almost your own.

Special element: The scar is the last living thread of Ser Aldous the Unfallen's first oath — the one he swore whole, generations before his first death, before it all started going wrong — carried down through blood he never knew he left behind. It has slept four lifetimes. The first time this character is dying and would not last the hour, the oath finishes what Aldous never could: it speaks once, in his exact final vow, through the character's own mouth, and it saves them — and from that moment binds them to end what Aldous became.
next_steps: Stakes: the oath fires without consent — not a power the character spends, a debt collecting itself. Once spent, the bloodline's last unbroken vow is gone. There is no second miracle.

Next steps: seed the scar and the taste of iron early, at low tiers, as flavour with no explanation. Pay it off here, at tier 4, the first time the character is truly at death's door. Ser Aldous — or word of him — should already be in the campaign before this fires, so the vow lands as recognition, not exposition.

Twists: before the debt ever collects, the character can seek out Ser Aldous, learn the oath's original wording, and speak it themselves — awake and willing — severing the inherited thread before it can bind them to his fate. Choosing *not* to go looking is itself a choice the table will feel later.

title: Drowned Kin
special_element: The nearest drowned thing has always been the same thing: your sibling, lost at sea three years ago. The compass isn't broken — it found them the day they went under, and it has never had reason to point anywhere else since.
summary: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 2 · Mood: grim
body: Category: Character Hook · Tier: 2 · Mood: grim

The needle on the Tidelace Compass never points north. Hold it and the brass is cold as bilgewater, the case ticking faintly against your palm — and for three years it has held one bearing, toward the nearest drowned thing, never once wavering however far you sail.

Special element: The nearest drowned thing has always been the same thing: your sibling, lost at sea three years ago. The compass isn't broken — it found them the day they went under, and it has never had reason to point anywhere else since.
next_steps: Stakes: Following the bearing means finally finding the body — or finding out it isn't a body anymore.
Next steps: The needle drifts for the first time this session, swinging a few degrees off true; something closer, and still moving, is now competing for "nearest drowned thing."
Twists: The bearing never moved because your sibling drowned once — it moved because they have gone on drowning, over and over, somewhere the party hasn't looked yet.
